news_summary: Today's space news highlights international collaboration with Jordan joining the Artemis Accords, recognition of leadership in space exploration, and new paleontological discoveries shedding light on ancient marine life.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale du jour met en avant la collaboration internationale avec l'adhésion de la Jordanie aux Accords Artemis, la reconnaissance du leadership en matière d'exploration spatiale, ainsi que de nouvelles découvertes paléontologiques qui apportent un éclairage sur la vie marine antique.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan la colaboración internacional con la adhesión de Jordania a los Acuerdos de Artemisa, el reconocimiento del liderazgo en la exploración espacial y los nuevos descubrimientos paleontológicos que arrojan luz sobre la vida marina antigua.

articles.title: Johnson Leaders Honored by National Space Club & Foundation
articles.summary: The National Space Club & Foundation announced its annual award recipients March 13, 2026, in Washington, D.C.   Two dedicated leaders from NASA’s Johnson Space Center were recognized for their contributions to human spaceflight.  Orion Program Manager Howard Hu received the Norman L. Baker Astronautics Engineer Award for sustained technical contributions to multiple human spaceflight efforts.   Hu leads […]
articles.content:
The 2026 award recipients at the 69th Annual Robert H. Goddard Memorial Dinner at the Washington Hilton on March 13, 2026. National Space Club & Foundation

The National Space Club & Foundation announced its annual award recipients March 13, 2026, in Washington, D.C.

Two dedicated leaders from NASA’s Johnson Space Center were recognized for their contributions to human spaceflight.

Orion Program Manager Howard Hu receives the Norman L. Baker Astronautics Engineer Award. National Space Club & Foundation

Orion Program Manager Howard Hu received the Norman L. Baker Astronautics Engineer Award for sustained technical contributions to multiple human spaceflight efforts.

Hu leads the design, development, production, and operations of Orion, NASA’s spacecraft for Artemis missions to the Moon. He has held several leadership roles within the Orion program, including deputy program manager, a manager of the Avionics, Power, and Software Office, and deputy manager of the Vehicle Integration Office. Hu has supported Orion since its inception, beginning as the Vehicle System Performance and Analysis lead.

On April 1, 2026, Artemis II launched on a 10-day voyage around the Moon, marking the first crewed flight of the Orion spacecraft. NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, and CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen splashed down safely inside Orion April 10 in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of San Diego. At their farthest point, the crew and spacecraft traveled 252,756 miles from Earth, setting a new record for the greatest distance traveled by humans in space.

The mission successfully proved the capability of Orion’s critical systems such as life support with humans aboard. Data from Artemis II will help refine mission operations and further evaluate Orion’s performance in deep space, supporting future Artemis missions.

NASA and its partners are now shifting their focus to Artemis III, which will test integrated operations between Orion and the human landing system in lunar orbit and advance plans to return astronauts to the Moon.

Before joining Orion, Hu served in multiple technical and leadership roles at Johnson, including chief engineer for exploration in the Aeroscience and Flight Mechanics Division, project manager and co-developer of shuttle abort flight management software for the Space Shuttle Cockpit Avionics Upgrades Program, and deputy guidance, navigation, and control system manager for the International Space Station program.

International Space Station Program Manager Dana Weigel receives the Eagle Manned Mission Award. National Space Club & Foundation

International Space Station Program Manager Dana Weigel received the Eagle Manned Mission Award. She leads development, integration, and operations for the International Space Station. The space station celebrated a historic milestone on Nov. 2, 2025, marking 25 years of continuous human habitation. The orbiting laboratory remains a critical testbed for future commercial destinations in low Earth orbit and for deep space exploration, supporting Artemis missions and future human missions to Mars.

Weigel has held several leadership roles within the program, including deputy chief of the Flight Director Office, where she led the Extravehicular Activity Recovery Team following a major in-flight spacewalk anomaly. She also served as a NASA flight director for STS-123 and led the agency’s geosynchronous Earth orbit satellite servicing habitat study.

NASA’s Johnson Space Center Director Vanessa Wyche attends the 69th Annual Robert H. Goddard Memorial Dinner with her husband George Wyche Jr. National Space Club & Foundation

Selected by panels of experts across industry, government, and academia, the awards reflect achievements that advance aerospace and national interests. Honorees were recognized at the 69th Annual Robert H. Goddard Memorial Dinner at the Washington Hilton.

“Dana Weigel and Howard Hu’s contributions to human space exploration, through their leadership and roles within the agency, are paramount,” said Johnson Director Vanessa Wyche. “It was a privilege to be there in person to celebrate and champion them as they were recognized for the lasting impact of their work. Congratulations to Dana, Howard, and all the award recipients on this well-deserved recognition.”

Hu and Weigel’s service exemplifies the leadership and technical excellence that continue to advance U.S. human space exploration.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights significant leadership contributions to human spaceflight, including the successful Artemis II mission which set a new record for human distance traveled in space and demonstrated critical spacecraft capabilities. The recognition of leaders advancing Artemis and ISS programs reflects meaningful progress in space exploration with broad scientific and societal impact.
articles.personality_title: NASA leaders honored for advancing human space exploration
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The National Space Club & Foundation holds an annual event to recognize people who have made important contributions to space exploration. This year’s ceremony took place on March 13, 2026, in Washington, D.C.

**What happened** – Two leaders from NASA’s Johnson Space Center received awards for their work. Howard Hu, the manager of the Orion spacecraft program, won the Norman L. Baker Astronautics Engineer Award. Dana Weigel, who manages the International Space Station (ISS) program, received the Eagle Manned Mission Award. Both were honored at the 69th Annual Robert H. Goddard Memorial Dinner.

**Impact** – Howard Hu has helped design and run NASA’s Orion spacecraft, which recently completed Artemis II, the first crewed mission to orbit the Moon. This mission set a new record by traveling 252,756 miles from Earth and proved Orion’s systems work well with astronauts onboard. Dana Weigel leads the ISS program, which celebrated 25 years of continuous human presence in space, providing a vital platform for testing technology that will help future missions to the Moon and Mars.

**What's next step** – NASA is preparing for Artemis III, a mission that will test how Orion works together with a lunar lander to bring astronauts to the Moon’s surface. The experience and data from Artemis II and the ISS will guide these future steps.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Two Johnson Space Center leaders were honored for their key roles in making NASA’s Artemis missions and the International Space Station successful and ready for future space exploration.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les dirigeants de la NASA récompensés pour leurs avancées dans l’exploration spatiale humaine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le National Space Club & Foundation organise chaque année une cérémonie pour récompenser les personnes ayant apporté des contributions importantes à l’exploration spatiale. Celle de 2026 s’est tenue le 13 mars à Washington, D.C.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Deux responsables du Johnson Space Center de la NASA ont reçu des prix pour leur travail. Howard Hu, responsable du programme du vaisseau Orion, a reçu le Norman L. Baker Astronautics Engineer Award. Dana Weigel, responsable du programme de la Station spatiale internationale (ISS), a reçu l’Eagle Manned Mission Award. Ils ont été honorés lors du 69e dîner annuel Robert H. Goddard.

**Impact** – Howard Hu a contribué à la conception et à la gestion du vaisseau Orion, qui a récemment effectué Artemis II, la première mission habitée en orbite lunaire. Cette mission a battu un record en parcourant 252 756 miles depuis la Terre et a démontré la fiabilité des systèmes d’Orion avec des astronautes à bord. Dana Weigel dirige le programme de l’ISS, qui a fêté 25 ans de présence humaine continue dans l’espace, servant de plateforme essentielle pour tester des technologies destinées aux futures missions vers la Lune et Mars.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA prépare Artemis III, une mission qui testera la coopération entre Orion et un module d’atterrissage lunaire pour ramener des astronautes sur la surface de la Lune. Les données d’Artemis II et de l’ISS guideront ces prochaines étapes.

**Phrase clé** – Deux dirigeants du Johnson Space Center ont été récompensés pour leurs rôles essentiels dans le succès des missions Artemis et de la Station spatiale internationale, préparant l’avenir de l’exploration spatiale.
articles.personality_title_es: Líderes de la NASA premiados por avanzar en la exploración espacial humana
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El National Space Club & Foundation organiza cada año un evento para reconocer a personas que han hecho importantes contribuciones a la exploración espacial. La ceremonia de 2026 se celebró el 13 de marzo en Washington, D.C.

**Qué pasó** – Dos líderes del Johnson Space Center de la NASA recibieron premios por su trabajo. Howard Hu, gerente del programa de la nave Orion, recibió el Norman L. Baker Astronautics Engineer Award. Dana Weigel, quien dirige el programa de la Estación Espacial Internacional (ISS), recibió el Eagle Manned Mission Award. Ambos fueron reconocidos en la 69ª Cena Anual Robert H. Goddard.

**Impacto** – Howard Hu ha ayudado a diseñar y manejar la nave Orion, que recientemente completó Artemis II, la primera misión tripulada que orbitó la Luna. Esta misión estableció un nuevo récord al viajar 252,756 millas desde la Tierra y demostró que los sistemas de Orion funcionan bien con astronautas a bordo. Dana Weigel lidera el programa de la ISS, que celebró 25 años de presencia humana continua en el espacio, proporcionando una plataforma clave para probar tecnologías que ayudarán a futuras misiones a la Luna y Marte.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA se prepara para Artemis III, una misión que probará cómo Orion trabaja junto con un módulo lunar para llevar astronautas a la superficie de la Luna. La experiencia y datos de Artemis II y la ISS guiarán estos futuros pasos.

**Frase clave** – Dos líderes del Johnson Space Center fueron premiados por sus roles clave para hacer exitosas las misiones Artemis y la Estación Espacial Internacional, preparando la exploración espacial futura.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two intertwined spacecraft silhouettes—one shaped like the Orion capsule orbiting a softly glowing crescent Moon, and the other resembling the International Space Station gently circling Earth—set against a serene starry sky with natural, muted blues, grays, and soft whites, symbolizing human leadership and achievement in space exploration.

articles.title: NASA Welcomes Jordan as 63rd Artemis Accords Signatory
articles.summary: The Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan signed the Artemis Accords Thursday during a ceremony hosted by NASA at the agency’s headquarters in Washington, becoming the latest nation to commit to responsible space exploration to benefit humanity.  “It is my privilege to welcome Jordan as the newest signatory to the Artemis Accords,” said NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman. “By signing the accords today, Jordan brings […]
articles.content:
Ambassador of the Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan to the United States Dina Kawar, center, signs the Artemis Accords alongside NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman, left, and U.S. Department of State Acting Principal Deputy Assistant Secretary for Oceans and International Environmental and Scientific Affairs Ruth Perry, right, on Thursday, April 23, 2026, at the Mary W. Jackson NASA Headquarters building in Washington. NASA/Keegan Barber

The Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan signed the Artemis Accords Thursday during a ceremony hosted by NASA at the agency’s headquarters in Washington, becoming the latest nation to commit to responsible space exploration to benefit humanity.

“It is my privilege to welcome Jordan as the newest signatory to the Artemis Accords,” said NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman. “By signing the accords today, Jordan brings valuable perspective and capabilities that will help expand the Golden Age of exploration for all mankind. They join at a pivotal moment, as we take the accords principles and put them into practice with humanity’s return to the Moon. Through Artemis, we’re going back to the lunar surface, with contributions from our international partners, to build a Moon Base and to stay.”

Ambassador Dina Kawar of Jordan signed the accords on behalf of the country. U.S. Department of State Acting Principal Deputy Assistant Secretary for Oceans and International Environmental and Scientific Affairs Ruth Perry also participated in the ceremony.

“Jordan has more engineers per capita than almost any country in the world,” said Kawar. “Through the National Council for Future Technologies, His Royal Highness Crown Prince Al Hussein is ensuring that talent has a direction, transforming Jordan into a regional and global technology hub across AI, digital infrastructure, advanced manufacturing, and now space. Today’s signing is proof that this ambition has no ceiling. We invite our American partners to build what comes next with us.”

In 2018, Jordan launched the JY1 satellite, a CubeSat developed by university students. The CubeSat transmitted images and audio from orbit after its launch on a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket from Vandenberg Space Force Base in California. Jordan’s growing interest in space includes a privately operated analog research facility in Wadi Rum, where the Jordan Space Research Initiative conducted its PETRA1 and PETRA2 missions in 2024 and 2025 to advance human spaceflight and planetary research for real-world benefits on Earth.

In 2020, during the first Trump Administration, the United States, led by NASA and the State Department, joined with seven other founding nations to establish the Artemis Accords, responding to the growing interest in lunar activities by both governments and private companies. The accords introduced the first set of practical principles aimed at enhancing the safety and coordination between like-minded nations as they explore the Moon, Mars, and beyond.

Signing the Artemis Accords means committing to explore peaceably and transparently, to render aid to those in need, to enable access to scientific data that all of humanity can learn from, to ensure activities do not interfere with those of others, and to preserve historically significant sites and artifacts by developing best practices for space exploration for the benefit of all.

More countries are expected to sign the Artemis Accords in the months and years ahead, as NASA continues its work to establish a safe, peaceful, and prosperous future in space.

Learn more about the Artemis Accords at:

https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-accords
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant international collaboration milestone in space exploration, with Jordan joining the Artemis Accords to promote responsible, peaceful, and cooperative lunar and space activities benefiting humanity. This has broad significance for global space governance and scientific progress, with detailed context about Jordan's growing space capabilities and the accords' principles.
articles.personality_title: Jordan joins Artemis Accords, advancing international space cooperation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Artemis Accords are a set of agreements created in 2020 by NASA and other countries to guide peaceful and responsible exploration of the Moon, Mars, and beyond. These agreements promote safety, sharing scientific knowledge, and protecting important sites in space.

**What happened** – On April 23, 2026, Jordan officially signed the Artemis Accords during a ceremony at NASA’s headquarters in Washington. Ambassador Dina Kawar signed on behalf of Jordan, with NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman and a U.S. State Department official present. This made Jordan the 63rd country to join the agreement.

**Impact** – Jordan’s signing is notable because the country has a growing space program, including engineers and scientists working on satellite projects and space research missions. Jordan has already launched a small satellite and conducted space research in the Wadi Rum desert. By joining the Artemis Accords, Jordan is committing to peaceful space activities and international cooperation, helping to shape the future of space exploration with others.

**What's next step** – As Jordan joins the group, NASA expects more countries to sign the Artemis Accords in the coming years. Together, these nations will work on missions to build a Moon base and explore space safely and fairly, sharing knowledge and protecting space environments.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Jordan’s entry into the Artemis Accords marks an important step in global teamwork for peaceful and responsible space exploration.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Jordanie rejoint les Accords Artemis et renforce la coopération spatiale internationale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les Accords Artemis, créés en 2020 par la NASA et plusieurs pays, définissent des règles pour une exploration pacifique et responsable de la Lune, de Mars et de l’espace lointain. Ces accords encouragent la sécurité, le partage des connaissances scientifiques et la protection des sites importants dans l’espace.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 23 avril 2026, la Jordanie a officiellement signé les Accords Artemis lors d’une cérémonie au siège de la NASA à Washington. L’ambassadrice Dina Kawar a signé pour la Jordanie, en présence de l’administrateur de la NASA Jared Isaacman et d’un responsable du département d’État américain. La Jordanie est ainsi devenue le 63e pays à rejoindre ces accords.

**Impact** – La signature de la Jordanie est importante car le pays développe rapidement son programme spatial, avec des ingénieurs et scientifiques travaillant sur des satellites et des missions de recherche spatiale. La Jordanie a déjà lancé un petit satellite et mené des recherches dans le désert de Wadi Rum. En rejoignant les Accords Artemis, la Jordanie s’engage à des activités spatiales pacifiques et à la coopération internationale, contribuant à façonner l’avenir de l’exploration spatiale.

**Prochaines étapes** – Avec l’arrivée de la Jordanie, la NASA prévoit que d’autres pays signeront les Accords Artemis dans les années à venir. Ensemble, ces nations travailleront à construire une base lunaire et à explorer l’espace de façon sûre et équitable, en partageant les connaissances et en protégeant les environnements spatiaux.

**Une phrase clé** – L’adhésion de la Jordanie aux Accords Artemis représente une avancée importante pour la coopération mondiale dans une exploration spatiale pacifique et responsable.
articles.personality_title_es: Jordania se une a los Acuerdos Artemis y fortalece la cooperación espacial internacional
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los Acuerdos Artemis, creados en 2020 por la NASA y varios países, establecen reglas para la exploración pacífica y responsable de la Luna, Marte y el espacio profundo. Estos acuerdos fomentan la seguridad, el intercambio de conocimientos científicos y la protección de sitios importantes en el espacio.

**Qué pasó** – El 23 de abril de 2026, Jordania firmó oficialmente los Acuerdos Artemis en una ceremonia en la sede de la NASA en Washington. La embajadora Dina Kawar firmó en nombre de Jordania, con el administrador de la NASA Jared Isaacman y un funcionario del Departamento de Estado de EE. UU. presentes. Jordania se convirtió en el país número 63 en unirse a estos acuerdos.

**Impacto** – La firma de Jordania es significativa porque el país está desarrollando rápidamente su programa espacial, con ingenieros y científicos trabajando en satélites y misiones de investigación espacial. Jordania ya ha lanzado un pequeño satélite y realizado investigaciones en el desierto de Wadi Rum. Al unirse a los Acuerdos Artemis, Jordania se compromete a actividades espaciales pacíficas y cooperación internacional, ayudando a moldear el futuro de la exploración espacial.

**Próximo paso** – Con la incorporación de Jordania, la NASA espera que más países firmen los Acuerdos Artemis en los próximos años. Juntos, estos países trabajarán para construir una base lunar y explorar el espacio de manera segura y justa, compartiendo conocimientos y protegiendo los entornos espaciales.

**Una frase clave** – La entrada de Jordania en los Acuerdos Artemis marca un paso importante en el trabajo conjunto global para una exploración espacial pacífica y responsable.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of three elegant hands—one adorned with a Jordanian bracelet, one with an American NASA patch, and one with a diplomatic ring—jointly holding a glowing, stylized crescent moon that gently illuminates a small, symbolic lunar base beneath it, set against a soft, natural-toned backdrop evoking unity and peaceful space exploration.

articles.title: Did kraken-like octopuses rule Cretaceous seas? Massive jaw fossils offer clues
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 23 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01342-wExtinct cephalopods might have been up to 19 metres in length and probably dined high up the food chain in ancient oceans.
articles.content:
Artist’s impression of an extinct Nanaimoteuthis species, often called krakens, which could have rivalled large marine reptiles in terms of size.Credit: Masato Hattori/Science Photo Library

In the age of the dinosaurs, while Tyrannosaurus rex terrorized the land, gargantuan octopuses might have been among the top predators in the sea.

Scientists have identified extinct octopuses — sometimes named krakens after the mythological monsters — that might have grown to nearly 19 metres in length. The estimate is based on fossilized jaws, which the researchers say show patterns of wear that came from devouring animals that had hard shells and skeletons.

Their study, published on 23 April in Science1, challenges the idea that giant marine reptiles such as mosasaurs and other vertebrates exclusively dominated marine ecosystems in the Cretaceous period 143 million to 66 million years ago. But other scientists say the largest size estimates of the octopuses — around the length of an articulated lorry — and any firm conclusion about their role in ecosystems should be taken with a pinch of salt.

Deep-sea denizens

‘Krakens’ have been identified from chitinous jaw fossils from the late Cretaceous . But their size range, diet and role in ecosystems were unclear, says study co-author Yasuhiro Iba, a palaeontologist at Hokkaido University in Japan.

To plug such gaps, Iba, together with Hokkaido palaeontologist Shin Ikegami and their colleagues, reanalysed 15 large fossil octopus jaws. They also identified 12 new kraken fossils through an analysis of carbonate rock layers assisted by artificial intelligence, an approach Iba’s team used to discover an explosion in squid diversity 100 million years ago2.

The analysis grouped the krakens into two species: Nanaimoteuthis jeletzkyi and N. haggarti, and discovered that they belong to the same evolutionary group as modern dumbo octopuses (Grimpoteuthis species).

On the basis of the anatomy of modern octopuses, the researchers estimate that the krakens’ mantles — the main, bag-like parts of their bodies — would have been between 67 and 443 centimetres long. Including the tentacles, N. jeletzkyi could have grown to between 2.8 and 7.7 metres in length, and N. haggarti from 6.6 metres to a whopping 18.6 metres.

“Some people will doubt that it really grew to 19 metres. I’m pretty sure about that,” says Christian Klug, a palaeobiologist at the University of Zurich in Switzerland, who was not involved in the research. The relationship between the mantle and tentacles of modern cephalopods is highly variable, so the lower or middle bounds of the size estimates could be more likely.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about extinct giant octopuses that challenges previous understanding of Cretaceous marine ecosystems. The findings provide new insights into ancient biodiversity and predator roles, which is inspiring and broad in scope for paleontology and natural history. The study uses advanced methods including AI analysis, adding substance and credibility.
articles.personality_title: Fossils reveal giant octopuses ruled ancient seas alongside dinosaurs
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – During the time of the dinosaurs, about 143 to 66 million years ago, the oceans were thought to be dominated by large marine reptiles like mosasaurs. Scientists were unsure about the size and role of giant extinct octopuses known as krakens.

**What happened** – Researchers studied fossilized octopus jaws found in rocks from the late Cretaceous period. Using artificial intelligence to analyze rock layers, they identified two kraken species related to modern dumbo octopuses. Based on jaw size and comparisons with living octopuses, they estimated these creatures could grow up to nearly 19 metres long.

**Impact** – This discovery suggests that giant octopuses were among the top predators in ancient seas, challenging the idea that large marine reptiles alone ruled these waters. The fossils show wear patterns indicating these octopuses ate animals with hard shells, showing their important role in the food chain.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue studying these fossils and look for more evidence to better understand how these giant octopuses lived and interacted with other sea creatures. The use of AI in fossil analysis may help find more hidden clues about ancient ocean life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Fossil evidence shows that enormous octopuses, some nearly 19 metres long, were powerful predators in Cretaceous seas alongside dinosaurs.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des fossiles révèlent que des pieuvres géantes régnaient sur les mers anciennes aux côtés des dinosaures
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – À l'époque des dinosaures, il y a environ 143 à 66 millions d'années, on pensait que les océans étaient dominés par de grands reptiles marins comme les mosasaures. La taille et le rôle des pieuvres géantes éteintes, appelées krakens, étaient inconnus.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié des mâchoires fossilisées de pieuvres trouvées dans des roches du Crétacé supérieur. Grâce à l'intelligence artificielle pour analyser les couches rocheuses, ils ont identifié deux espèces de krakens liées aux pieuvres dumbo modernes. En comparant la taille des mâchoires à celle des pieuvres actuelles, ils ont estimé que ces animaux pouvaient atteindre près de 19 mètres de long.

**Impact** – Cette découverte suggère que des pieuvres géantes faisaient partie des prédateurs dominants dans les mers anciennes, remettant en question l'idée que seuls les grands reptiles marins régnaient sur ces eaux. Les fossiles montrent des traces d'usure indiquant qu'elles mangeaient des animaux à coquilles dures, soulignant leur rôle important dans la chaîne alimentaire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques poursuivront l'étude de ces fossiles et chercheront davantage de preuves pour mieux comprendre comment ces pieuvres géantes vivaient et interagissaient avec d'autres créatures marines. L'utilisation de l'IA pourrait aider à découvrir plus d'indices cachés sur la vie océanique ancienne.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des fossiles montrent que des pieuvres énormes, certaines proches de 19 mètres, étaient des prédateurs puissants dans les mers du Crétacé aux côtés des dinosaures.
articles.personality_title_es: Fósiles revelan que pulpos gigantes dominaron los mares antiguos junto a los dinosaurios
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante la época de los dinosaurios, hace entre 143 y 66 millones de años, se creía que los océanos estaban dominados por grandes reptiles marinos como los mosasaurios. Se desconocía el tamaño y papel de los pulpos gigantes extintos llamados krakens.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores estudiaron mandíbulas fosilizadas de pulpos encontradas en rocas del período Cretácico tardío. Usando inteligencia artificial para analizar las capas rocosas, identificaron dos especies de krakens relacionadas con los pulpos Dumbo actuales. Comparando el tamaño de las mandíbulas con pulpos vivos, estimaron que podían crecer hasta casi 19 metros de largo.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo sugiere que los pulpos gigantes fueron depredadores importantes en los mares antiguos, desafiando la idea de que solo los grandes reptiles marinos dominaban esos ecosistemas. Los fósiles muestran marcas de desgaste que indican que comían animales con conchas duras, mostrando su papel destacado en la cadena alimentaria.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando estos fósiles y buscarán más pruebas para entender mejor cómo vivían estos pulpos gigantes y cómo interactuaban con otras criaturas marinas. El uso de IA en el análisis fósil puede descubrir más pistas sobre la vida en los océanos antiguos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Evidencias fósiles demuestran que pulpos enormes, algunos de casi 19 metros, fueron depredadores poderosos en los mares del Cretácico junto a los dinosaurios.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a colossal, ancient octopus with long, flowing tentacles gracefully navigating deep Cretaceous seas alongside silhouettes of large marine reptiles, set against softly colored underwater rock formations and subtle fossil imprints, evoking a majestic and mysterious prehistoric ocean world.
